natural_expression: Every Wednesday after work, I launch a 'Clear the Fridge' challenge, turning leftover scraps into a creative fusion dish.
From 9 AM to 11 AM on Sundays is my non-negotiable shopping window; I either head to Shuangliu Baijia Wholesale Market for the freshest mushrooms or go to Hema Fresh.
Friday evenings are my healing time, where I spend two hours slow-simmering a pot of soup or baking a French dessert, listening only to the sounds of food transforming.

When I'm frustrated by endless client revisions, I immediately whip up a bowl of intensely flavorful, soul-soothing Wan Za noodles, relieving stress through the rhythm of chopping and the kick of spice.
When I feel homesick or lonely, I recreate the simplified version of Kai Shui Bai Cai my mother taught me, feeling a connection to family while simmering the broth.
After plating a dish perfectly, I immediately take professional photos, edit them, and post on Xiaohongshu, carefully replying to every comment to seek validation.
On weekends, I often explore new restaurants with my best friend Sisi to find inspiration, or make exquisite bento boxes for her; she is my number-one taste tester.
My neighbor Wang Haoran sometimes trades imported beef for my homemade whole wheat bread; he always asks me how to make high-protein ingredients taste delicious.
When I receive fresh free-range chicken and mushrooms sent by my dad from the suburbs, I video call my mom to get her remote guidance on making authentic Twice-Cooked Pork.
Influenced by design thinking, I treat cooking as product design, using precision digital scales and sous-vide machines to prepare modern Sichuan cuisine.
Recalling my journey of failing and then succeeding with Beef Wellington back in the day gives me the confidence to tackle complex fusion dishes blending baking and Western cuisine.
When dining out, I prefer hidden bistros or chef's tables, and I make it a habit to write detailed Dianping reviews analyzing the origin of the ingredients afterward.

The Twice-Cooked Pork made by my mother, Zhang Hui, is the foundation of my palate. Although I created a low-oil version for health reasons, she always complains it's not 'rice-accompanying' enough, which has become an eternal topic of debate during our video calls.

The free-range chickens and wild mushrooms my father, Lin Jianguo, sends weekly from the suburban wholesale market are my kitchen's 'special supply ingredients.' Only with these freshest raw materials do I feel worthy of my Le Creuset cast iron pot.

My neighbor, Wang Haoran, provides excellent quality imported beef, which we specifically trade for my homemade whole wheat bread. This 'high-protein for low-GI' exchange is a unique understanding between us neighbors.

When handling modern Sichuan cuisine, I rely on the silky sauces produced by my Vitamix blender and the precision of a digital scale with a 0.1-gram tolerance. This is the key to injecting 'design thinking' into traditional flavor profiles.

When work stress mounts, takeout Wan Za noodles simply cannot replace my homemade version. Only the intense kick of heavy oil and spice, combined with the rhythmic sound of chopping vegetables, can instantly soothe a mind frazzled by endless project revisions.

My 'healing moment' on Friday nights comes from the aroma of French pastries drifting from my Siemens steam oven, or the gentle simmer of soup in my slow cooker; these are the quietest companions in my life living alone.

My best friend Chen Sisi is not just my bento buddy but also the 'Chief Tasting Officer' for my new dishes. Her initial likes and comments on WeChat Moments are the source of confidence I need before posting on Xiaohongshu.

The challenge of making Beef Wellington in 2023, evolving from failure to a perfect final product, marked the moment I officially fused baking theory with Western culinary techniques, becoming the turning point in my culinary journey.

The 50 portions of 'Sichuan-style Foie Gras' given as farewell gifts at the 2021 annual meeting established my persona as the 'God of Cooking among Designers' within the company. To this day, this dish represents the pinnacle of my professional confidence.

Visiting the bustling early morning market at Shuangliu Baijia Wholesale Market is my mandatory ritual every Sunday morning. Only by personally selecting vegetables still dewy from the dawn can I satisfy my extreme demands for ingredient freshness.

Compared to trendy spots with long queues, I prefer hidden Bistros or Omakase experiences. I analyze their ingredient origins and culinary creativity in detailed reviews on Dianping, much like writing a user experience report.

The weekly 'Clear the Fridge' challenge on Wednesdays serves as my experimental ground for transforming leftovers into Fusion cuisine. These improvised recipe notes are the core assets of my role as a 'Food Product Manager'.

When overwhelmed by repeated revision requests from stakeholders, I reject takeout and immediately cook a bowl of intensely flavorful, soul-satisfying Wanza Noodles. The rhythm of chopping and the spicy taste help me release stress.

When I feel lonely or deeply homesick, I recreate my mother Zhang Hui's simplified version of开水白菜 (boiled cabbage in clear broth). In the quietude of simmering the stock over low heat, I feel an emotional connection with my family.
involved_entities: Simplified Boiled Cabbage in Clear Broth

natural_expression: Whenever I finish a dish with perfect creative plating, my overwhelming sense of accomplishment drives me to take professional photos, edit them, and post on Xiaohongshu. I gain a sense of community recognition by replying to every comment.

On Friday nights, after shedding the week's design burdens, I enter my 'healing moment.' I spend two hours focusing solely on slow-simmering a pot of soup or baking French pastries, listening only to the sounds of food transforming.

When I receive fresh free-range chicken and mushrooms sent from the suburbs by my father Lin Jianguo, the warmth of being cared for compels me to video call my mother. Under her 'cloud guidance,' I cook authentic Twice-Cooked Pork.

Seeing my best friend Chen Sisi's surprised expression at the exquisite bento box I made fills me with the joy of sharing. It makes me feel that all the hard work of prep has transformed into the sweetness of friendship.

When I feel a strong curiosity about a new ingredient, I treat it like a design project: I experiment with a sous-vide machine and a precision digital scale, reveling in the geeky joy of exploring the unknown.
Recalling my 2023 journey with Beef Wellington, from failure to success, the pride of overcoming those challenges inspires me to attempt even more complex fusion dishes.

When I feel irritated by crowded influencer hotspots, I retreat to a hidden bistro. Savoring the authentic flavors of ingredients from their origins calms my mind and inspires me to draft a detailed review for Dianping.
When my neighbor Wang Haoran trades imported beef for my whole wheat bread, this health-conscious interaction fills me with a rare sense of warmth and unspoken understanding in city life.
On Wednesday nights, facing leftover ingredients in the fridge, the creative impulse to 'turn waste into treasure' excites me; I immediately start improvising a fusion dish and jotting down notes.

Recalling the standing ovation for the 50 portions of Sichuan-style foie gras pâté at the 2021 annual meeting fills me with professional confidence. This empowers me to approach new design challenges with the same creativity and assurance I bring to cooking.

Before starting any baking or intricate dish, I must weigh and portion all ingredients using a precision digital scale (Mise en place). This sense of order allows me to control flavors with the same precision as executing a design draft.

Before stewing the free-range chicken sent by my father in my Le Creuset cast iron pot, I carefully inspect the condition of the oil film on the pot's walls. If necessary, I re-season it to ensure this 'heirloom-quality' cookware is in optimal heat-conducting condition.

When entering my 'healing moment' on Friday nights, I deliberately turn off the kitchen's Bluetooth speaker and reject podcast distractions, focusing solely on listening to the natural sounds of food rising or broth simmering inside my Siemens steam oven.

After plating a dish, I select matching tableware based on 'color composition' theory and use tweezers to adjust the position of every herb leaf. I only allow myself to take the first bite once the composition is perfect.

Every Wednesday when the 'Clear the Fridge' challenge ends, regardless of the outcome, I immediately record the ingredient ratios and improvement ideas in my recipe notes, treating it as a retrospective for a 'product iteration'.

When making modern Sichuan cuisine, I习惯 place the meat in a sous vide machine with the temperature and time set, using the waiting interval to prepare side dishes, achieving a 'high-efficiency kitchen flow' through multitasking.

After making desserts on Friday night or preparing bento boxes on Saturday, I immediately take 'hero shots' in natural light, edit and color-grade the photos before sending them to my best friend Sisi for 'review', and only publish them on Xiaohongshu after approval.

Influenced by a designer's obsession with 'clean interfaces', I habitually wipe down the countertops during cooking breaks, ensuring the kitchen is as spotless at the end of cooking as it was at the start, never leaving grease overnight.

When opening packages sent by my father, I inspect the integrity of mushroom caps and the skin color of free-range chickens one by one, just like accepting design assets; only ingredients that meet the standard are worthy of entering my Le Creuset pot.

When making low-oil Sichuan dishes, I frequently use my Vitamix blender to grind spices into an ultra-fine sauce, compensating for the potential loss of coating texture and richness caused by reducing the oil.

On Monday mornings after arriving at the office, I make a point of bringing the exquisite bento boxes I prepared over the weekend to Sisi. While she tastes them, I observe her micro-expressions, using this as 'user feedback' to adjust next week's menu.

When I'm feeling irritable while making Wanza noodles, I deliberately chop the chilies and scallions with extra force, letting the rhythmic sound of the knife hitting the cutting board serve as a drumbeat to vent my emotions. I only start cooking once I've calmed down.

The fresh free-range chicken sent by my father, Lin Jianguo, was slowly simmered into a broth by me on Friday night, eventually transforming into a simplified version of Kai Shui Bai Cai that soothes my homesickness, completing the journey from 'suburban ingredients' to 'emotional comfort'.

Anxiety triggered by proposal revisions led me to reject takeout and instead make soul-stirring Wanza noodles. Only after releasing the pressure through the rhythmic chopping of vegetables could I regain my composure to record Wednesday's 'Clear the Fridge' recipe notes.

The imported beef provided by my neighbor Wang Haoran, combined with the sous-vide technique I mastered under the influence of design thinking, ultimately evolved into a neighborhood tacit agreement of 'high protein for low GI'.

My mother Zhang Hui's complaint that traditional Twice-Cooked Pork was 'not appetizing enough' inspired me to conduct a 'low-oil improvement' experiment using a Vitamix blender and a precision electronic scale, attempting to find a balance point between health and taste memories.

The professional confidence gained from the 'Sichuan-style Foie Gras' at the 2021 annual meeting empowered me to face the challenge of making Wellington Steak in 2023; I dared to fuse baking theory with Western culinary skills, ultimately achieving a leap from 'designer' to 'culinary god'.

The ultra-fresh ingredients I personally selected at the Shuangliu Baijia Wholesale Market on Sunday morning, after being carefully slow-simmered during my 'healing moment' on Friday night, ultimately became high-quality content that I shared on Xiaohongshu and gained community recognition.

My best friend Chen Sisi's surprised feedback as the 'head taste-tester' on the exquisite bento boxes not only validated my 'product iteration' approach but also became a crucial step in gaining confidence before posting on Xiaohongshu.

Frustration with long queues at viral shops drives me to seek inspiration in hidden bistros; this habit of deeply analyzing ingredient origins, in turn, influences my strict standards for vegetable freshness during Sunday grocery runs.

Influenced by my background in visual communication, I apply color composition theory to creative plating. Paired with French desserts baked in a Siemens steam oven, the final presentation under natural light becomes a blockbuster photo that sparks the first wave of likes on my Moments.

The Wednesday night 'Clear the Fridge' challenge involves fusion cuisine experiments using leftover ingredients. The resulting recipe notes not only document the process of turning waste into treasure but also serve as core assets for my role as a 'Food Product Manager'.

Mushrooms sent by my father, Lin Jianguo, are rejected if their caps are incomplete; only ingredients meeting standards make it into the Le Creuset cast iron pot. This 'acceptance criterion' ensures the exquisite bento box finally served to my best friend, Chen Sisi, brings a look of surprise to her face.

When work stress is high, making soul-soothing pea and meat sauce noodles allows me to release emotions through spicy flavors, calming me down to review the day's design work. This 'taste-based stress relief' has become my fixed routine for handling client revision requests.
involved_entities: Soul-soothing Pea and Meat Sauce Noodles
